entry.title: Re: Issue with Employee Status Picklist in Event reason FO
entry.content:
This is due to your Profile Language settings you have set in your instance. If you have English US and English GB available and only maintained English_GB in the label, make sure that your langugage settings of your profile (Profile>Settings) is set to English_GB language too, else you will see blank data. 

Other task you need to do is maintain all translation of the label so you won't have to change your langugae settings

entry.title: Re: Unable to create ABAP trail instance in BTP trail acc...
entry.content:
Hi Lokesh,

In the trial landscape, you can have only one ABAP environment instance per global account. This error typically occurs if:

  1. An existing ABAP instance is already created (even if partially failed).
  2. Entitlements are not correctly assigned to the subaccount.
  3. The previous instance is stuck in 'Creation failed' state.

In trial account, quota limitations are strict, and sometimes cleanup is required before creating a new instance.

Regards,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Re: Career Advice: BRIM vs IS-Utilities for an ABAP Consultant
entry.content:
Hi there,

Great question — and it’s good that you’re thinking about your long‑term direction this early in your career.

Since you already have 3 years of ABAP experience in SAP CRM, both BRIM and IS‑Utilities are realistic paths, but they lead to very different types of work.

If you’re looking for faster market demand and broader opportunities: 👉 IS‑Utilities (IS‑U) is the safer and more stable choice. Utilities companies in North America and Europe are actively modernizing, moving to S/4HANA, and adopting BTP extensions. IS‑U functional consultants are in high demand, and your ABAP background will help you understand billing, device management, and FI‑CA processes quickly.

If you’re interested in high‑value niche consulting: 👉 BRIM is excellent, but it’s more specialized. BRIM projects are usually in telecom, media, subscription‑based industries, and large enterprises. The learning curve is steeper (CI, FI‑CA, SOM, CC), but the roles are well‑paid and often more architecture‑focused.

A simple way to decide:

  • If you enjoy industry processes (billing, metering, field service, customer service), choose IS‑U.
  • If you enjoy complex charging, rating, and subscription models, choose BRIM.

From a career perspective:

  • IS‑U → easier transition, more openings, strong demand with S/4 migrations
  • BRIM → fewer roles, but higher specialization and strong long‑term value

Both paths can take you into a functional or techno‑functional role, depending on your interest.

Hope this helps you make an informed decision.

entry.title: Re: Security deposit interest document creation from invoice
entry.content:
Hi,

You’re correct that Event 2015 (Interest Calculation: Create Document) is the right enhancement point, but as you’ve noticed, it fires for all interest‑creation scenarios — mass billing, FPINTM2/FPI2, and any custom program. So using it directly will stop interest creation everywhere, not just during the mass invoice run.

To control this only for the mass invoice job execution, you need a way to differentiate the calling process. Here are a few practical options based on my best understanding that are commonly used in IS‑U projects:

  1. Identify the Mass Billing Run Using the Billing Schema / Invoicing Context

During mass billing, the system sets specific runtime parameters (e.g., invoicing run ID, schema, or job name). You can read these in Event 2015 and conditionally skip interest creation only when the call originates from the mass billing process. Batch date and BATCH ID from EA38.

This is the cleanest approach because it avoids touching other processes.

  1. Use a Custom Switch (Z‑Parameter) Read by Event 2015

If your mass invoice job is triggered via a custom wrapper or variant, you can set a custom flag (e.g., in memory, TVARVC, or a Z‑table) before the run starts.

Event 2015 checks the flag → skips interest creation → resets after job completion.

This gives you precise control without impacting FPINTM2/FPI2.

  1. Check the Calling Program / Sy‑CPROG

Mass billing typically runs under specific programs such as:

  • REAINV00
  • REABILLB
  • REAINV01 (depending on version)

Inside Event 2015, you can check sy-cprog or cl_abap_get_call_stack to determine whether the call originated from the mass invoice run.

If yes → suppress interest creation If no → allow normal behavior

  1. Use a BAdI Instead of Event 2015 (If on S/4HANA)

if nothing work than the last option,  In S/4HANA, some customers prefer using:

  • BADI_FKK_SE_INT_CALC
  • BADI_FKK_SE_DOC_CREATE

These give more granular control and are easier to manage than classic events.

Hope this help, if not let me know, will try to see since I did similar in my project.

entry.title: Re: Configure based on Customer types
entry.content:
Hi,

Here’s how these scenarios are typically handled in IS‑U/AMI integrations:

  • Excluding customers with errors or zero‑consumption billing This is usually controlled in the MDUS/AMI extraction logic. Most utilities use a filtering step (either in the AMI middleware, MDUS BAdIs, or a custom selection program) to skip:
  1.  contracts with billing errors
  2. zero‑consumption bills
  3. accounts in dunning or special statuses You simply add these conditions in the selection criteria before generating the AMI request.
  • Sending only on‑cycle customers to AMI This is done by checking the billing class / billing cycle during the extraction. On‑cycle contracts are selected, and off‑cycle contracts are excluded. Many customers implement this via MDUS BAdIs or a custom wrapper around the AMI request creation.
  • Handling demand meter changes mid‑month When a demand meter is exchanged mid‑period, the system typically uses periodic register reads and selects the highest demand value across the old and new meters for that billing period. This is standard behavior in most MDUS/AMI integrations, but can also be controlled via custom logic if needed.

entry.title: Re: EA19 Document date check
entry.content:
Hi,

There’s no standard configuration in IS‑U to prevent users from entering a future Document Date in EA19. The transaction accepts any date as long as it’s a valid posting date in FI‑CA and this is very common requirement across all project I work.  its logically correct as well,  since we should not allow posting of financial document for future date since in between the timeframe anything happen like posting of payment or cancellation.  

If you need to enforce this rule, the usual approach is to implement a small check in the billing events, most commonly:

  • Event R402 / R403 (Billing document creation checks), or

  • Event 2000 / 2015 (FI‑CA document creation checks), depending on where you want the validation to happen.

Inside the event, you can compare the entered document date with sy-datum and raise an error if it’s greater than today.

There’s no standard switch or config for this, so an event enhancement is the recommended way.

Now the great Q is how do you handle your ORT/PT business cases, for those type of cases please apply a check in TVARVC, if that's check is enable update the date to current else keep it same. 

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: Automate BPEM Case Resolution Using Standard Configuration
entry.content:
Hi,

Standard SAP does not provide a way to automatically resolve BPEM cases end‑to‑end without any user action. I know it differ from previous response but still please read why I said so.

BPEM is intentionally designed as an exception‑handling framework, so SAP expects either a user decision or a custom automation layer (BAdIs, events, or external workflow) to close cases. Out‑of‑the‑box, there is no configuration switch that auto‑resolves cases created by EMMA.

Regarding EMMAJP → Execute Solution Processes: This button triggers the Solution Process assigned to the BPEM case category. A Solution Process is essentially a predefined follow‑up action (e.g., trigger a workflow, run a program, call a function module). It must be configured in:

IMG → SAP Utilities → Customer Service → BPEM → Define Solution Processes

If no solution process is assigned to the case category, the button will not perform any action. If a process is assigned, EMMAJP can execute it for multiple cases in mass.

To summarize:

  • Auto‑closing BPEM cases is not possible via standard; it requires enhancement.
  • “Execute Solution Processes” runs the configured follow‑up action for the case category.
  • You can maintain these processes in the BPEM configuration under Solution Processes.

Hope this helps. Let me know if you need the config path or BadI names, Basically auto closure rule is key since its setup for each case category individually.

entry.title: Re: How to navigate from RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER to FQEVENTS
entry.content:
Hi,

This is expected behavior. When you run EA26 directly, the billing program calls the FI‑CA event framework in dialog mode, so your breakpoint in Event R401 is hit immediately.

However, when you trigger the same process through RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER, the event is executed in a background job, not in the foreground session you are debugging. Because of that, your normal breakpoints will never be reached.

To analyze R401 during a mass invoice job, you have a few options:

  1. Use an external breakpoint (user‑specific) Set an external breakpoint in your R401 FM and make sure the background job runs under your user ID. Only external breakpoints are respected in background processing.
  2. Switch the job to run in “Debug Mode” In SM37, select the job → Job → “Debugging” before it starts. This forces the job to run in debug mode and will stop at your breakpoints.
  3. Insert a temporary hard stop As a last resort, add a temporary BREAK-POINT ID <id> or MESSAGE … inside R401 to force the debugger to open when the background job hits that code.
  4. Confirm that your variant actually triggers the same billing program RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER only schedules the mass activity. The actual billing is executed by the underlying mass activity program (e.g., REAINV00). Make sure your variant is correctly linked to EA26’s mass activity.

In summary: Your event is being triggered, but because the mass run executes in background, your normal breakpoints won’t fire. Use external breakpoints or debug the job directly to trace the call into R401.

entry.title: Re: Need help understanding meter reading reason 11 and 16
entry.content: Reason 11 is typically created as a pre‑programming meter read, while Reason 16 is generated after the programming activity is completed. Because the system records the initial read first and the confirmation read afterward, you usually see Reason 11 entries before Reason 16 in the meter‑reading sequence.

entry.title: Re: due date for credit memos FKK_CREATE_DOC
entry.content: When you use FKK_CREATE_DOC, the system treats credit memos as incoming payments from an FI‑CA perspective. Because of that, the due‑date logic follows the rules for receivables, not payables. Credit terms for outgoing payments are not evaluated here. This is standard FI‑CA behavior, not a limitation of the function module.

entry.content:

Best!

entry.title: Re: How do you manipulate the values of STXH, COBL, VBPA...
entry.content:
During VA01(Sales Order creation) there is no single user-exit to directly manipulate STXH, COBAL and VBPA together.

  1. VBAP - User save related exits in MV45AFZZ (like USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE or USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT) and modify partner buffer table (XVBPA).
  2. COBL - Normally handled through configuration (OKB9 /derivation rules). If enhancement is required, user FI substitution or BADI. It is not purely controlled in SD exits.
  3. STXH: Use text determination or handle in MV45AFZZ save exit before the document is saved.

Always check configuration first before using exits. 

Regards,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Re: VA01 Item Overview Sorting
entry.content: In standard VA01, the item overview is always sorted by POSNR. You cannot change the sorting to another VBAP field through configuration.

entry.title: Re: ABAP : Compiled language or Interpreted language
entry.content: ABAP is compiled to Program Code and then interpreted by the SAP runtime system.

entry.title: Securing SAP Agentic AI Across Architectural Boundaries
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity and Compliance Advisor. As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as AI Security & governance, cloud security, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit and compliance.)

Introduction

 Enterprise AI is entering a new phase, shifting from deterministic automation to agentic autonomy, where AI systems can reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows with minimal human intervention. In the SAP ecosystem, this evolution is enabled by SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and the SAP AI Core runtime, which together provide a scalable, tenant-isolated, hyperscaler-agnostic foundation for running agentic capabilities in an enterprise context.

These agents increasingly operate as digital coworkers, making real-time decisions, retrieving context, and interacting with business-critical processes and enterprise data. But with greater autonomy comes a new security reality: exposure to prompt injection, goal misalignment, hallucinations cascading into actions, privilege misuse, and tool-driven execution risks that traditional application security models were not designed to address. This blog walks through a secure, end-to-end architecture for SAP Agentic AI, highlighting the key control points needed to make autonomy both powerful and defensible.

This blog follows the end-to-end request lifecycle of an SAP Agentic AI interaction from the moment a user submits a query to the final audit trail. Each of the nine steps maps to a specific architectural layer, with its risks, data handling, and compliance controls examined inline. Think of it as walking alongside a request as it flows through the system.

Secure Agentic AI Architecture Flow

Figure 1 shows the complete request lifecycle of an SAP Agentic AI interaction. Each numbered step represents a distinct architectural boundary with its own security controls, data handling rules, and compliance requirements. The flow follows a request from the moment a user submits a query through Fiori, Joule, or a custom agent, all the way through identity verification, AI processing, business execution, and finally the forensic audit trail.

The following sections provide a detailed write-up for each step. Each step includes a breakdown diagram showing the internal flow, components involved, risks controlled, and the data that transitions between layers.

Figure1: Secure End-to End Agentic AI Flow

Step 1: User Request — Entry Point

The lifecycle begins when a user interacts with SAP Fiori, Joule Copilot, or a custom/third-party application. At this stage, the request already carries identity context (JWT is typically established via OAuth/OIDC flows and passed to BTP apps). The primary risk here is impersonation or malicious input submission. Mitigation relies on strong authentication controls such as SSO, MFA, and encrypted transport (TLS 1.2/1.3). From the first interaction, identity integrity and session security are enforced to prevent unauthorized access.

Figure 2: User Request: Entry FlowFigure 2: User Request: Entry Flow

Step 2: Identity & Access Control — Zero-Trust Enforcement

Once the request enters the platform, SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS) and OAuth-based authorization validate the identity and enforce role-based access control. XSUAA issues scoped, short-lived JWT tokens to ensure least-privilege execution. The risk at this layer is privilege escalation or token misuse. Mitigation comes from RBAC, scoped JWTs, and strict principal propagation, ensuring the AI agent acts strictly within subset of the user’s permissions preventing Confused Deputy attacks and ambient authority.

NOTE: SAP is evolving toward IAS + AMS for centralized, attribute-based authorization, while XSUAA remains supported for existing deployments.

Figure 3: Zero-Trust Enforcement

Step 3: Gen AI Hub — Inbound Policy Check

 Before any model is invoked, the request passes through the Gen AI Hub’s inbound control layer. This stage governs prompts as code using a Prompt Registry, applies input filtering to mitigate prompt injection, and masks PII before external model interaction. The key risk here is injection or data exfiltration via manipulated instructions. Mitigation includes version-controlled prompts, sanitization, and privacy-preserving preprocessing to ensure only policy-compliant inputs reach inference.

                                

Figure 4: Inbound Policy Check

Step 4: Trusted Data Retrieval — Hybrid RAG Grounding

To reduce hallucination risk, the system retrieves contextual enterprise data using a hybrid RAG approach. This includes semantic retrieval via the HANA Cloud Vector Engine and structured reasoning via a Knowledge Graph. Retrieval is authorization-aware, enforced via HANA Cloud Row-Level Security (RLS), ensuring the AI only accesses user-permitted context. The main risks are hallucination and unauthorized data exposure. Mitigation relies on controlled retrieval pipelines and strict enforcement of access controls during context assembly.

Figure 5: Hybrid RAG Grounding

Step 5: LLM Inference — SAP AI Core Runtime

Model inference executes inside SAP AI Core, which provides tenant-isolated runtime environments within SAP AI Core, using resource group segmentation based on Kubernetes namespaces. This prevents cross-tenant leakage and isolates workloads, while ensuring customer data is never used for foundation model training. Risks at this stage include model bias, model integrity concerns, or unintended data leakage. Mitigation is achieved through runtime isolation, encryption in transit and at rest, and enterprise-grade infrastructure controls. The runtime handles thinking — not governance or execution.

Figure 6: SAP AI Core Runtime

 Step 6: Gen AI Hub — Outbound Response Validation

After inference, responses pass back through the Gen AI Hub for post-processing. This stage applies output filtering, consistency validation against grounding sources, and controlled PII reconstruction for authorized recipients. The primary risk is cascading hallucination or unsafe output propagation. Mitigation includes policy filters and confidence checks that prevent unreliable outputs from triggering downstream actions or misleading users.

Figure 7: Outbound Response Validation

Step 7: MCP Gateway — Execution Boundary

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) Gateway, running within SAP Integration Suite/API Management, acts as the execution boundary between AI reasoning and business system action. It enforces tool allow-lists, per-tool authorization checks, and optional Human-in-the-Loop approval for sensitive operations. The key risk here is unauthorized transactions or tool misuse. Mitigation is achieved through scoped JWT enforcement, tool-level authorization validation, and workflow-based approvals before any system change occurs. This is relatively new capability reflecting SAP's evolving architecture, as MCP adoption is still early-stage in enterprise deployments.

Figure 8: MCP Gateway

Step 8: Backend Execution — Enterprise Systems

Once authorized, the action is executed via Standard APIs or Business Events in systems like SAP S/4HANA, adhering to Clean-Core principles and existing ERP controls. This stage operates under existing ERP controls and clean-core principles. The primary risks are financial misstatement, operational disruption, or regulatory breaches. Mitigation relies on established enterprise safeguards such as segregation of duties (SoD), transaction logging, and change management controls already embedded in core systems.

Figure 9: Backend Execution

Step 9:  Audit Logs — End-to-End Traceability

The final layer captures full traceability across the lifecycle: identity → proposal → decision → action → outcome. Audit logs are integrated with monitoring systems and retention policies to ensure forensic-grade investigation capability. The main risk at this stage is non-repudiation failure or investigation gaps. Mitigation comes from structured logging, monitoring, and evidence retention mechanisms that support regulatory and internal audit requirements.

Figure 10 : End to End Traceability

Data Processed across Layers

To complement the architectural flow, the table below maps each step of the SAP Agentic AI lifecycle to the type of data processed, what (if anything) is stored, the sensitivity classification, and the key controls applied. This view shifts the discussion from architecture to governance — clarifying how identity, prompts, embeddings, transactions, and audit traces are handled across the pipeline.

Rather than treating agentic AI as a single system, this breakdown highlights the distinct control layers: identity enforcement, orchestration governance, grounded inference, execution boundary controls, backend safeguards, and audit traceability. Understanding what data is processed and retained at each stage is critical for compliance, risk assessment, and regulatory alignment.

Step

Layer

Data processed

Data stored

Sensitivity

Key controls

1–2

Identity

User claims, JWTs

Session audit logs

PII

short-lived JWT

3

Inbound Gate

Prompts, PII (masked)

Prompt versions, filter logs

Business-Critical

PII masking, customer data is not used for 3rd party model training

4

Grounding

Embeddings, SPARQL queries

Vectors, ontologies, CDS

Confidential

Tenant isolation, auth-aware

5

LLM Inference

Sanitized prompts + context

None (stateless)

Masked

TLS 1.2+, customer data is not used for model training

6

Outbound Gate

Validated responses

Confidence logs

Business-Critical

Output filtering, PII reconstruction

7

MCP Gateway

Actions, tool params

Activity logs

Transactional

HITL, Audit Trail

8

Backend

ERP transactions

Transaction logs

Financial

ERP controls, SoD

9

Audit

Full event chain

BTP and Conversation Insight logs

Regulatory

Alignment to EU AI Act, GDPR,

Conclusion:

Securing agentic AI is not a single control — it is an architectural discipline. The nine-step SAP Agentic AI lifecycle shows why: identity enforcement, prompt governance, grounding, isolated inference, output validation, execution control, backend safeguards, and auditability each play a distinct role, and no layer can compensate for a missing one.

What makes the architecture defensible is how AI-specific safeguards compose with proven enterprise patterns: SAP Cloud Identity Services enforce zero-trust access, the Generative AI Hub orchestrates governed prompts, input/output controls and masking, grounding leverages HANA Cloud’s vector/semantic capabilities with authorization-aware retrieval, SAP AI Core provides tenant-isolated inference runtime under enterprise data protection terms, and the MCP Gateway via Integration Suite creates a policy-controlled execution boundary before any business action. The result is autonomy within enforceable trust boundaries, supporting auditability and governance expectations (e.g., GDPR, EU AI Act, ISO 42001) as organizations move from AI experimentation to controlled business process execution.

Disclaimer:

© 2026 SAP SE or an SAP affiliate company. All rights reserved. See Legal Notice on www.sap.com/legal-notice for use terms, disclaimers, disclosures, or restrictions related to SAP Materials for general audiences.

entry.title: Re: ABAP OOP
entry.content:
In real SAP projects, ABAP OOP is widely used, especially in S/4 HANA, RAP, FIORI and BADI Implementation.

  • Global classes are used for reusable business logic and framework (Preferred in real projects).
  • Local classes are used when the logic is specific to one report/program.

So, it depends on the requirement, but enterprise development mostly relies on global classes.

To level up, practice separating business logic into service classes and avoid logic directly in reports.

Best Regard,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Inconsistent Production Version Determination Between Costing Run and Production Order – SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
We are observing inconsistent production version determination behavior in SAP S/4HANA.

System Details:

  • System: SAP S/4HANA 2025

  • Two valid production versions exist simultaneously

Both production versions:

  • They are valid for the date range in which production is carriedout

  • Are marked for costing

  • Have valid BOM and routing assignments

During CK40N/CK11N (Costing Run), the system selects Production Version V002.

During CO01 (Production Order Creation/Converting planned order into a production order), the system selects Production Version V001. There is no manual selection; the system determines the version automatically.

Due to this reason, there is a considerable amount of variance recorded during the variance calculation. Is there a standard way to pick the production version automatically during the production order creation (or planned order creation), which is already there in the costing run for that particular period? 

Regards,

Oshan.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Unleashing Artificial Intelligence for Regulatory Insights with SAP
entry.content:
You may recall a GRC Tuesdays blog released some time ago (cf. Implications of AI in the field of Regulatory Compliance) where I mentioned a Customer Influence program investigating the use of artificial intelligence in the area of regulatory intelligence and internal control.

Well, I have good news: Engineering teams from across SAP have come together for an end-to-end compliance platform and have now released a new SAP Business Technology Platform service for regulatory insights natively integrated with SAP Process Control for end-to-end regulatory intelligence.

 

Why the need for an automated service?

 

According to analysts, there are over 300 regulatory changes every day. Of course, not all these updates will impact your organization, but you may still need to check them to assess the (lack of) impact. In many organizations, this is done manually internally or outsourced to specialized 3rd party providers. And there is therefore a cost in monitoring these updates. Especially since the business impact assessment is usually performed by legal subject matter experts.

Furthermore, the identified regulatory changes then need to be reflected into your internal control framework or gaps can occur between the regulation and the controls being tested… which would be problematic.

As a result, digitalization becomes a key objective for compliance surveillance to reduce the cost, but also to increase the efficiency and resiliency of the control framework itself to ensure legal requirements are continuously met, and that nothing falls through the cracks.

 

 Regulatory Compliance

 

Jointly leveraging multiple artificial intelligence areas such as text mining, natural language processing and large language models, the new service for regulatory insights automatically analyses and highlights risk and control changes in legal texts, best practice procedures or policies and suggests updates to existing controls or even new control definitions if needed. It also offers analytical insights into control coverage and gap analysis as well as recommendations to reduce the gap.

 

How does it work?

 

Unlike a magician that never reveal its tricks, there is no magic here – and Regulatory Insights certainly doesn’t function as a black box. So it is transparent and more importantly auditable.

 

Upload document & automatic parsing

User uploads relevant document (regulatory framework or control standard, internal policy, etc.) which gets parsed and stored.

 

Automatically detect & extract control definitions

 Regulatory Insights automatically detects and extracts control definitions into a structured format.

 

Document red-line analysis & control delta analysis

 Control definitions are compared between versions to flag, add, subtract and/or change deltas.

 

Control mapping (not in scope of 1st release but coming soon)

 New control definitions are mapped to any similar existing ones to empower joint implementation.

 

Dashboard with Gap & Coverage Analytics (not in scope of 1st release but coming soon)

 Analytics dashboard for gaps and coverage of (mapped) controls are visualized together with action recommendations.

 

Updated content in SAP Process Control

 Thanks to dedicated SAP Fiori applications, information from regulatory insights can be leveraged in SAP Process Control to create, map and adjust the internal controls. Simply select the Regulatory Insights Framework, the associated Regulation in SAP Process Control and create new controls or map to existing ones! This ensures a seamless flow from the regulatory intelligence to the control operations.

 

Unlock business benefits

 

In summary, Regulatory Insights enables companies to automatically detects changes in regulatory documents, maps changes to controls, and suggests recommendations to mitigate risks for non-compliance. With this service, organizations can:

 

Mitigate and reduce risk of non-compliance, business disruption and reputational loss​

 

Boost efficiency and speed up time-to-action by lowering the cost and effort for regulatory compliance

 

Make informed business decisions by leveraging smart technologies

 

More to come!

 

This release already supports a variety of regularory frameworks – including various versions of CELEX, SAP Security Baseline Template, COSO, ESRS, C5, COBIT, FitSM, GHB, MaRisk, NIST Security & Privacy (NIST SP 800-53), IT Act, SOX, and Experimental - which allows end users to upload and test requirement extraction on additional regulations that are not yet officially supported. But the team is continuously evaluating further frameworks and working towards a generic solution to extract compliance requirements from a larger variety of document sources.

 

Interested in learning more?

 

If you’d like to dive a little deeper here and hear more about it before it’s in general availability, I recommend the following resources:

  • Blog: Compliance in the Age of AI: How SAP GRC’s Regulatory Insights is Redefining Risk Management
  • Webcast: SAP GRC 2026 Deep Dive: Regulatory Insights in SAP GRC

Finally, if you would like to participate as a customer to the Early Adopter Care program, feel free to register. The campaign is open until June 23rd 2026: Smarter Regulatory Analysis with AI : SAP Regulatory Insights Early Adopter Launch

What about you, how does your organization manage regulatory surveillance? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Re: Fixed amounts missing in 0CO_OM_CCA_1_D (Cost and Allocation) for target costs
entry.content: In RSA3 for datasource 0CO_OM_CCA_1_D, fixed amounts for allocations (Umlage) with VTYPE = 030 often don’t appear because the extractor doesn’t populate them from CO tables in the same way as percentage-based allocations. The values may exist in ECC (COEP, COSP, COSS), but they aren’t transferred by this datasource. If you need them, you’ll likely have to check the underlying tables directly or enhance the extractor, since by design 0CO_OM_CCA_1_D doesn’t deliver fixed amounts for target costs.

entry.title: Re: Project Budget Report - Budget (AVC) Value
entry.content:
Good day @sans16 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, you are not missing a configuration step. The key point why you face that limitation is, in Public Cloud, the AVC budget values are not always exposed in a released CDS view that you can directly reuse in your custom report the same way you can for actuals and commitments. Budget and available budget are often calculated and exposed through standard analytical content the logic defined in a app. See Budget Availability Control Profile for more insight.

This basically means, for your report, you should build your custom report with released data source ( Actuals, Commitment, Withheld commitments, etc.), reuse available standard budget reporting content for AVC, etc.

Regarding you question on where to fetch Total Budget (AVC), artifacts such as View Browser, Custom CDS Views and released API catalog is the decision path to consider. If not, I suggest you keep the custom app for actuals and commitments, stick to budget from standard analytical budget content instead of trying to reconstruct and present available budge either from standard output or from a controlled replicated dataset. This basically adhering to the public cloud’s clean core approach.

This Blog provides some insights on CDS Views: Explore SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, CDS Views on SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Product Division in SAP S4 HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi All,

Currently customer has 1 sales org. with 4 divisions. Products are segregated as per the division. Sales Org. (1000) having 4 different divisions (10, 20, 30, 40) for sales and 2 distribution channels (wholesale and Retail (newly added)). As per customer requirement, wholesale distribution channel cannot sell product across divisions in single sales order, but retail distribution channel must allow selling products across different divisions in a single sales order.

In Product Master if we maintain the division in basic data then same is extended to Sales org. as well applicable to all distribution channels. This will prevent selling products in Retail distribution channel when products from different divisions required in single sale order. If Division not maintained in the Product master, then system will not restrict selling products across the division in wholesale distribution channel. We have proposed creation of separate division (00-Common) used for Retail sales and maintenance of Product exclusion at sales area level. But customer is highlighting lot of data maintenance required for the exclusion. Any better suggestion will be helpful.

entry.title: Finance in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
Hello finance gurus! I’m thrilled to guide you through the exciting new finance innovations delivered in our 2602 release.

This update is packed with features designed to bring more intelligence, automation, and insight into your finance operations. We're continuing our journey to help you streamline core processes, enhance your analytical capabilities, and make faster, more data-driven decisions.

If you’re looking for a quick summary of the top Finance release highlights for version 2602 – check out my video.

For a full overview of all the release highlights, please see the table of contents below.

Let's dive into the highlights!

 

Video 1. Release Highlights for Finance in SAP Cloud ERP 2602

Table of contents

  • Embrace Intelligent Automation with AI
    • Accounting Accruals Agent
    • Joule for SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity
  • Deeper Insights with Next-Generation Analytics
    • Credit Line Analysis - Review Booklet
    • Actual Liquidity Analysis - Review Booklet 
    • Enhanced Reporting for Product and Service Margins
  • Streamlining Your Core Finance and Treasury Operations
    • New Foundation for Collection Processes
    • Flexible Partial Retirements in Asset Accounting
    • Innovations in Cash and Liquidity Management
      • Simplified Bank Account Master Data Distribution
      • More accurate short-term cash positioning
      • Introducing Cash Snapshots
  • Expanding the Financial Ecosystem for Complex Business Models
    • Universal Revenue Recognition (URR) for Solution Business
    • Innovations on the Horizon for SAP Green Ledger
      • Carbon tracking for projects and WBS elements
      • Accounting for Carbon Allowances (CBAM)
      • AI-Assisted Carbon Emission Analysis with Joule
  • Finance Intelligence Data Products for Business Data Cloud
  • Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!
  • Find all our assets

This update highlights significant innovations in the following areas:


Embrace Intelligent Automation with AI

Artificial intelligence is no longer a future concept; it's a practical tool embedded directly into your core finance processes. In the 2602 release, we are introducing powerful AI capabilities to reduce manual effort and accelerate your operations.

<back to top>

Accounting Accruals Agent

Say goodbye to the time-consuming and manual process of calculating and posting accruals at period end. Our new agentic AI for accruals does the heavy lifting for you. It interprets your accounting policies, analyzes historical data, and generates accrual proposals. As a general ledger accountant, you are notified when proposals are ready, and you can quickly review and post them in the new Manage Accrual Proposals app, complete with detailed reasoning for each calculation. This significantly streamlines your accrual management and frees up your team for more strategic tasks.

This feature is going to be made generally available in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.1.

Video 2. Accounting Accruals Agent (opens in a new page)

 

Video 3. Accounting Accruals Agent - demo

Value Proposition

  • Significantly reduce time spent in calculating and preparing accrual postings at period end
  • Significantly reduce time reviewing the calculated accrual amounts and posting the journal entries
  • Streamline accrual management processes by transitioning from manual preparation and posting of general journal entries to an efficient, automated process.

Capabilities 

  • Automate Accruals with AI: Leverage agentic AI to generate accrual proposals for manual accruals by interpreting accounting policy documents, retrieving historical data, and performing the required calculations.
  • Instant Notification for Accountants: Notify the general ledger accountant when the accrual proposals are ready for review.
  • Streamlined Review Process: Enable the general ledger accountant to quickly and more simply review and post the proposed accrual journal entries in the new Manage Accrual Proposals app.
  • Transparent Calculations: Provide a detailed reasoning for the accrual amounts calculated by the AI agent and the proposed journal entries in the new Manage Accrual Proposals app.

For more information see: Discovery Center and Business Value 3-Pager

<back to top>

Joule for SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity

Finding the right information when you need it is crucial. We’ve integrated Joule, your AI copilot, with SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity. Now, you can ask questions about features, configuration, or integration scenarios in natural language. Joule searches the SAP Help Portal, analyzes the content, and provides you with clear, summarized answers, saving you time and streamlining access to relevant knowledge.

Picture 1. SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity: using Joule to access solution-related content

Value Proposition

  • Improve user satisfaction through the ability to express business requirements in natural language
  • Enable quicker access to user-enablement content summarized in a crisp answer
  • Save time by streamlining access to relevant information

Capabilities 

  • Quick overview: search and summarize SAP Help Portal documentation for a quick understanding of processes and features of SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity
  • Direct Access to Resources: provided links to original documentation for further deep-dive

For more information, see: Joule with SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity | SAP Help Portal

<back to top>

Deeper Insights with Next-Generation Analytics

The ability to analyze data from multiple dimensions is key to understanding business performance. With this release, we're expanding our powerful Review Booklet framework and enhancing our margin analysis reporting. Review Booklets represent the next generation of multidimensional analytics, creating a consistent, intuitive user experience.

<back to top>

New Review Booklets for Cash Management:

Credit Line Analysis - Review Booklet (F9064)

This new app enhances how you analyze credit line data with a superior user experience, more flexible querying, and embedded AI capabilities for generating insightful overviews. It provides the functionality of the Credit Line Analysis app (W0051) in the review booklet framework. It includes dedicated pages for utilization, transactions, and forecasts. You can generate several AI-assisted overviews for each of the tabs.

Picture 2. Credit Line Analysis - Review Booklet

Value Proposition

  • Improve the user experience by enhancing visualization and enabling faster response times
  • Enhance analysis capabilities with more-flexible querying for multidimensional insights
  • Provide an embedded AI capability

Capabilities 

  • Improved user experience: migrate the classic Credit Line Analysis app to an enhanced Review Booklet app for a better user experience
  • Extended capabilities: add multiple dedicated pages, including:
    • Credit Line Overview
    • Credit Line Utilization Report
    • Credit Line Analysis Transactions
    • Credit Line Analysis by Currency/Counterparty/Company Code
    • Forecast of Credit Line
  • AI-driven analytics

For more information, see: Credit Line Analysis – Review Booklet.

<back to top>

Actual Liquidity Analysis - Review Booklet (F7923)

Gain clear insights into your liquidity situation. This app presents your cash flow data in a standardized liquidity item hierarchy, showing opening and closing balances, highlighting unrealized FX effects, and summarizing past periods to identify where cash comes from and how it's used.

 

Video 4. Actual Liquidity Analysis

Value Proposition

  • Actual liquidity analysis of cash flows in bank accounts
  • Determining the sources and usages of cash flows
  • Empowered by Review Booklets

Capabilities 

  • Cash flow at a glance: view structured cash flow balance by fiscal year period in Transaction Currency and Display Currency.
  • Track FX Variances Easily: view Unrealized FX Difference for each fiscal year period.
  • Access to detailed information: Navigate to the Check Cash Flow Items app to analyze cash flow item details.

For more information, see: Actual Liquidity Analysis

<back to top>

Enhanced Reporting for Product and Service Margins (F9104)

The new version of the Product and Service Margins app provides a richer, more flexible multi-dimensional analysis experience. You can now analyze revenue, costs, and margins with support for multiple currencies (company code and global) and a new sales deduction measure, all while leveraging customizable charts and layouts for more intuitive reporting. 

Picture 3. Product and Service Margins app (F9104)

Value Proposition

  • Gain comprehensive profitability insights across various processes and objects
  • Enhance data visualization with intuitive charts for seamless reporting
  • Gain greater flexibility and relevance for diverse business needs through customizable reporting

Capabilities

  • Analysis on multiple levels: multidimensional analysis of profitability metrics across various objects such as service orders, sales orders, projects, solution orders, and provider contracts
  • Dynamic data visualization: visualization of profitability data interactively with customizable charts featuring series axis and export options
  • Tailored reporting: reports with custom fields to market segments

For more information, see: Product and Service Margins (New Version)

<back to top>

Streamlining Your Core Finance and Treasury Operations

Were constantly refining core processes to improve efficiency, control, and flexibility. The 2602 release brings significant enhancements across receivables, asset, and cash management.

<back to top>

New Foundation for Collection Processes

Improved functionality for receivables management (scope item 7MA) helps you collect receivables faster and minimize the impact of outstanding items. A prioritized customer worklist is the central starting point for agents, while automated processes and rule-based frameworks help automate team assignments and keep data in sync with AR accounting.

Picture 4. Process Worklist in Collections and Dispute Automation (F8832)

​Value Proposition

  • Minimizes operational and financial impact of outstanding receivables on operations and profitability
  • Swift collection processes leads to higher customer satisfaction and loyalty, positively impacting brand reputation
  • Actionable insights from analytics and real-time integration with accounts receivable help companies make informed decisions

​Capabilities

  • Central hub: the worklist with prioritized customers is the central starting point for collections agents.
  • Comprehensive account insights: collection accounts provide a comprehensive overview by combining key figures, information from master data, and related objects.
  • Collaborative collection tools: use tasks and activities as collaborative tools to facilitate internal collection workflows.
  • Track customer agreements: Keep track of agreements with your customers by recording promises to pay and disputes.
  • Tailor customer prioritization: use provided rules to prioritize customers according to your requirements.
  • Automate team assignments: apply the rules framework to automate team assignments and set business blocks.
  • Sync processes automatically: automated background processes keep data and organizational structures in sync with accounts receivable accounting.

For more information, see: Collections and Dispute Automation

<back to top>


Flexible Partial Retirements in Asset Accounting

You now have more control over partial asset retirements. In the Post Asset Retirement app (F7107), you can choose a processing sequence that mirrors a LIFO (Last In, First Out) or FIFO (First In, First Out) method, deciding whether to retire balances from the current year first or from prior years first.

Picture 5. Post Asset Retirements (F7107)

​Value Proposition

  • Decide whether you want to retire older or newer asset balances first
  • Define the processing sequence for partial retirements by choosing a LIFO or FIFO-like option

​Capabilities

  • Based on the chosen option, the system considers the respective balance amounts from the different fiscal year when making the retirement posting. The following new options are now available:
    • Current Year First, Then Prior Years: The system first considers balance amounts resulting from acquisitions of the current year, then ones from previous years. This option corresponds to the LIFO (Last In First Out) method in inventory accounting.
    • Prior Years First, Then Current Year: The system first considers balance amounts of previous years, then ones from the current year. This option corresponds to the FIFO (First In First Out) method in inventory accounting.

For more information, see: Processing Sequence Depending on the Year of Acquisition

<back to top>


Innovations in Cash and Liquidity Management

Simplified Bank Account Master Data Distribution

For organizations using a Treasury Workstation, you can now configure system groups to streamline the distribution of bank account master data, ensuring only relevant information is replicated to target systems.

Picture 6. System groups for bank accounts

​Value Proposition

  • Centralized management offers a unified view and easier maintenance of bank account distribution across systems.
  • Significantly reduces complexity in system mapping configurations through BAdIs in receiver systems, thereby reducing IT involvement and associated costs
  • Enhanced efficiency with automated house bank account conversion.
  • Flexibility is provided through configuration for direct copying or conversion of remote house bank accounts, tailored to specific business needs.

​Capabilities 

  • Simplified system management: system group configuration for easy management of systems in the Treasury Workstation setup
  • Targeted data replication: during data replication, the system automatically identifies and sends only the bank accounts that are relevant to a specific remote system by filtering based on house bank account connectivity.
  • Seamless account conversion: automatically converts house bank accounts from remote systems to operative ones when they are replicated to the target systems.
  • Account details: additional attributes, such as general ledger account numbers, are provided for remote house bank accounts.

For more information, see: System Groups for Bank Account Master Data Distribution.

<back to top>

More accurate short-term cash positioning

The Short-Term Cash Positioning app now supports the opening available balance from intraday bank statements, providing a more accurate starting cash position for better-informed treasury decisions.

Picture 7. Short-term cash positioning

​Value Proposition

  • A  more accurate starting cash position by using the opening available balance to reflect the funds can be spent at the beginning of the banking day.
  • Better-informed treasury decisions based on more up-to-date fund availability rather than relying on the previous day's closing balances
  • A seamless and reliable cash position is achieved by using intraday bank statements as the source of opening balances, with a built-in fallback to the prior day's closing balance when intraday bank statements are not available.

​Capabilities

  • New option to use opening available balances as the source of opening balances in the Define Cash Position Profiles app.
  • Consumption of opening available balances in the Short-Term Cash Positioning app.
  • Fallback mechanism that uses end-of-day balances when intraday bank statements are not available.

For more information, see: Integrating Intraday Opening Available Balances in Short-Term Cash Positioning.

Introducing Cash Snapshots

With the new Manage Cash Snapshots app (F8962), you can capture a snapshot of your cash-related data (like cash flows and balances) at a specific timestamp. This allows you to track and analyze historical cash data, review past decisions, and identify late-posting transactions.

Picture 8. Manage Cash Snapshots app (F8962)

​Value Proposition

  • Capture cash position snapshots for future review and decision justification.
  • Help users to identify late-posting transactions and discrepancies.

​Capabilities

  • Capture cash snapshots manually or via scheduled jobs.
  • Centrally manage cash snapshots in one app.
  • Delete cash snapshots that are no longer needed.
  • Load captured snapshots for analysis using the Short-Term Cash Positioning app.

For more information, see: New Apps for Cash Snapshots and Cash Snapshots in Short-Term Cash Positioning.

<back to top>

Expanding the Financial Ecosystem for Complex Business Models

As business models evolve, so must your financial software. This release adds critical support for solution-oriented business and enhances data accessibility.

<back to top>

Universal Revenue Recognition (URR) for Solution Business

URR now supports solution orders that combine sales orders, customer projects, and service or subscription contracts. This enables you to automate revenue recognition for these complex multi-element arrangements, ensuring IFRS 15 compliance and providing powerful analytic reporting for a complete accounting perspective.

 

Video 5. Universal Revenue Recognition (URR) for Solution Business

​Value Proposition

  • Enable customers to automate revenue recognition for solution orders to adhere to IFRS15 compliance rules

​Capabilities

  • Complex arrangements possible: enable multi-element arrangement across the various item types of a solution order
  • Allocation flexibility: for the allocation, use a standalone selling price for sales, service contracts, subscriptions, and fixed-price customer project items
  • Insightful revenue analytics: provide analytic reporting for solution business from the accounting perspective, such as waterfall reporting for revenue forecasts and allocation impact

For more information, see: Universal Revenue Recognition for Bundling with Solution Orders | SAP Help Portal.

<back to top>

Innovations on the Horizon for SAP Green Ledger

The increasing demand for transparency in environmental impact reporting requires a new level of financial and non-financial data integration. The SAP Green Ledger is our answer, embedding carbon accounting directly into the core of your financial system. Heres a look at the powerful innovations on our roadmap that will redefine how you manage sustainability.

<back to top>

Carbon tracking for projects and WBS elements

To truly understand your carbon footprint, you need to track emissions at a granular level. We've enhanced the Green Ledger to allow for the posting and analysis of carbon quantities directly on projects and WBS elements. This will give you profound insights into the emissions generated by specific services, investments, and customer-facing projects.

 

Video 6. Carbon tracking for projects and WBS elements

Value Proposition: Improve decision-making by understanding the climate impact of your projects, enable accurate reporting on services delivered to customers, and gain a holistic view of emissions from all business activities.
Capabilities to Look Forward To: Replicating project structures, collecting carbon quantities on WBS elements, and analyzing this data seamlessly within your finance system.

For more information, see Import Master Data.

<back to top>

Accounting for Carbon Allowances (CBAM)

Regulatory requirements are evolving, and starting in 2026, the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) will mandate that companies account for liabilities from embedded carbon in imported goods. To ensure you are prepared, we are developing capabilities for accounting for carbon allowances within a dedicated carbon journal.

 

Video 7. Accounting for Carbon Allowances (CBAM)

Value Proposition: This will help you comply with EU Regulation 2023/956 and other greenhouse gas accounting rules (like US GAAP and IFRS guidance), ensuring auditability and sufficient coverage of emissions with allowances.
Capabilities to Look Forward To: Tracking embedded carbon in imported goods, accounting for emission quantities and allowances, and preparing the necessary financial postings for both gross and net models.

For more information, see Carbon Certificate Management.​

<back to top>

AI-Assisted Carbon Emission Analysis with Joule

Data is only as valuable as the insights you can derive from it. We are bringing the power of our generative AI assistant, Joule, to the Green Ledger. With AI-assisted carbon emission analysis, you'll be able to generate charts and visualizations from your carbon data using simple conversational prompts.

 

Video 8. AI-Assisted Carbon Emission Analysis with Joule

Value Proposition: Reduce the time and effort needed for carbon reporting, gain instant insights to support reduction strategies, and make sophisticated data analysis accessible to a broader range of users.

Capabilities to Look Forward To: Generating various chart types by simply asking Joule, analyzing dimensions like GHG scope and category, and even asking follow-up questions for deeper analysis.

For more information, see SAP Green Ledger, carbon emission analysis in SAP Discovery Center .

<back to top>

Finance Intelligence Data Products for Business Data Cloud

We are delivering over 75 primary data products for Finance in the Business Data Cloud. These building blocks covering areas like accounting, financial close, cash, and group reporting ensure your finance data is available to build analytical apps with near real-time insights and make data-driven decisions.
<back to top>

This is just a glimpse of the many innovations in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2602 release. Our goal is to empower you with the tools you need to run an intelligent, efficient, and agile finance organization.

I encourage you to explore these features further by visiting the linked resources in the SAP Help Portal and checking out the SAP Roadmap Explorer.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Artificial Intelligence as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

<back to top>

🔎 Find all our assets 

Do you want to learn more about the main innovations in different lines of business in the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release? The release 2602 showcases more 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

Enablement

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below: 

<back to top>

Find all enablement assets and update blog post for Finance: Finance for SAP S/4HANA Cloud – The Collection

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Also, visit the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: Alina Keilbach | LinkedIn

entry.title: Re: country assignment to tax procedure
entry.content:
Options to Resolve

Keep Address in Canada
If the vendor/customer is meant to transact under the Canadian company code, their master data address should be Canadian (so the Canadian tax procedure applies). You can still add a “communication address” in China, but the main address must align with the company code’s tax procedure.

Extend Canadian Tax Procedure to China (Not Recommended)
Technically, you could assign TAXCAJ to China in OY01, but this would break compliance because China has its own tax rules. This is not a valid approach for real business/legal scenarios.

Use Partner Functions / Alternative Address
Maintain the vendor/customer with a Canadian address (so TAXCAJ applies). Add a Chinese correspondence address for logistics/shipping purposes. This way, tax jurisdiction remains consistent with the Canadian company code.

Customizing Alternative
If the business truly requires Chinese addresses under Canadian tax jurisdiction, you’d need a custom solution (e.g., enhancement to allow jurisdiction assignment independent of country). But this is unusual and typically avoided because it conflicts with SAP’s standard tax logic.

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; APAC)
entry.content:
PLEASE NOTE: Selecting the ‘Will you be attending?’ option will NOT register you for the event.

To register for this event, please click here: Register Here

SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; APAC)

This session will help you understand the relevant components of an adequate adoption program to maximize your cloud investment and ensure sustained business value. We will walk you through the essentials to orchestrate Change Management, Governance, Sustainment, and Value Management and how they work together to drive ongoing adoption, solution optimization and user experience.​ Participants will explore best practices and take away actionable templates to build an initial transformation roadmap, define clear roles and responsibilities, and identify the right executive sponsors and stakeholder groups.​ This webcast is a foundational session that will lead into optional follow‑on topic specific webcasts on your preferred topics.. At the end, you will leave with a concrete structure and toolkit to start or refine your own SuccessFactors adoption program.

Please contact SAP_SuccessFactors_Onboarding@sap.com for any questions on this event.

Here is a link to our Global schedule

entry.title: Re: VAT code with base amount different from invoice amount
entry.content:
@Aliice 

FTXP: 

Remember about mapping in OBZT and FIPL_SAFT for new tax code.

OBYZ - I created new confition types: 

- ZBAS (base amount),

- Z001 (Input tax mod.base - ESE)

-Z002 (No deduct.mod.base - NAV)

 

Then, I modified TAXPL procedure:

- added step 105 - ZBAS

- added step 145 - Z002 deriving base amount from step 105

- added step 215 - Z001 deriving base amount from step 105

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Getting to a Clean Core - The new maturity model of extensions into four distinct levels (A,B,C,D)
entry.content:
Clean Core Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA

From 3‑Tier Model to Refined Extensibility Levels (A–D)

Clean Core Extensibility Definition Refinement

Introduction

Considering changing business and technology landscape, future‑proofing ERP systems is no longer optional—it is essential. SAP customers are facing constant disruptions driven by new technologies, geopolitical shifts, evolving business models, and customer expectations.

This blog help us understand why Clean Core matters, what SAP changed in the extensibility model, and how customers and partners should design extensions going forward, using the refined Clean Core Extensibility Levels (A–D).

Agenda:

  • Why Clean Core matters
  • Embrace the change
  • The contradiction: Standardization vs Differentiation
  • Clean Core principles and dimensions
  • Refined extensibility levels (A, B, C, D)
  • On‑stack vs Side‑by‑Side extensibility
  • Governance, KPIs, and Clean Core journey

Why Clean Core Matters?

Organizations fail not because they are weak, but because they cannot adapt fast enough. Large enterprises often struggle to change direction quickly due to deeply embedded, hard‑coded customizations in their ERP systems.

Let us understand using Transformation Headwinds:

Summary : Businesses must continuously respond to:

  • Process complexity
  • New technologies (AI, automation, analytics)
  • Business model changes
  • Supply chain disruptions

When business processes are tightly coupled to SAP core code, adapting to change becomes slow, risky, and expensive.

Now how to Embrace the Change

  1. Be Up‑to‑Date – Upgrade Readiness          

           SAP continuously invests in innovation. To benefit from:

    • Process improvements
    • Better user experience
    • AI and automation  

                 

Customers must stay on the latest release.
This is automatic in Public Cloud, but in Private Cloud, upgrades are customer‑managed and heavily impacted by custom code quality.

Standardization is the key enabler for smooth upgrades.

    2. Be Adaptable – Run Agile     

         Businesses also need differentiation:

    • Custom processes
    • Automation
    • Optimized workflows

         

However, differentiation traditionally comes through custom development, which increases technical debt if done incorrectly.

The Core Contradiction:                     

  • Standardization → Upgrade stability
  • Differentiation → Business Value

      

✅Clean Core is SAP’s strategy to balance both.

And What Is Clean Core ?

Clean Core refers to a set of guiding principles that support:

  • Continuous transformation
  • Agile, innovative ERP systems
  • Decoupled extensions
  • High data quality and integration     

The Five Clean Core Dimensions:              

  • Business Processes – Reduce complexity
  • Extensibility – Decouple custom code
  • Data – Controlled, high‑quality data
  • Integration – Stable, API‑based integration
  • Operations – Reliable and efficient system operation

 

👉This blog focuses primarily on Extensibility.

Evolution of the Extensibility Model 

4 Levels : 

        

High Level Recomendations :    

  • Avoid binary “clean / not clean” decisions
  • Better assess legacy custom code
  • Provide practical guidance for customers and partners

        

SAP evolved the 3‑Tier Extensibility Model into a refined level‑based model to:

  

Clean Core Extensibility Levels (A–D)

✅Level A – Clean Core (Best Practice)

  • Publicly released SAP APIs
  • SAP guarantees stability
  • Includes:
    • Side‑by‑Side extensions on SAP BTP
    • On‑stack ABAP Cloud extensions

👉Highest upgrade stability

✅Level B – Clean Core (Next Best Option)

  • Classic APIs (e.g., BAPIs, stable frameworks)
  • No formal stability contract, but SAP‑recommended

👉Still considered Clean Core

⚠️Level C – Conditionally Clean

    • Usage of SAP internal objects
    • No official SAP recommendation
    • Allowed only if no A or B option exists

SAP mitigates risk using a Change Log:

    • Tracks incompatible changes per release
    • Helps assess upgrade impact via ATC

👉Acceptable, but requires monitoring

❌Level D – Not Clean Core

  • Modifications to SAP standard
  • Direct write access to SAP tables
  • Implicit/explicit enhancements

👉Must be avoided or refactored

From 3‑Tier Model to Level Concept

Earlier:

  • Tier 1 – Cloud / Side‑by‑Side
  • Tier 2 – Wrapper‑based
  • Tier 3 – Classic ABAP

Now:

  • Legacy ABAP is differentiated into A, B, C, and D
  • Enables better upgrade stability assessment

Qualifiers :

     

Type C Tracking Via Change Log Approach : 

    

    

When to use which extensibility Model :

  

On‑Stack vs Side‑by‑Side Extensibility

On‑Stack

Used when:

  • Transactional consistency is required
  • Heavy SQL/data proximity is needed
  • Key User Extensibility is sufficient

Includes:

  • Key User tools
  • Developer Extensibility (ABAP Cloud)

Side‑by‑Side (SAP BTP)

Used when:

  • Extension lifecycle should be independent
  • Mobile or external users are involved
  • CI/CD isolation is required
  • Non‑ABAP skills (Java, Node.js, CAP)

Includes:

  • SAP Build
  • CAP
  • ABAP Environment on BTP

👉Always Level A

Choosing the Right Extension Path

SAP provides:

  • Decision trees
  • SAP Application Extension Methodology

This helps:

  • Identify business value
  • Select extension style
  • Choose the right technology building block

Examples / Use case :

How To Measure Clean Core :

      

Clean Core Measurement KPIs at a glance :

Measuring Clean Core Maturity

SAP introduced a Governance & Maturity Framework with:

  • 12 extensibility practices
  • Scoring from 0 to 5
  • Integrated into RISE with SAP methodology

Clean Core KPIs Examples 

  • Clean Core Share – Distribution of A/B/C/D
  • Technical Depth Score – Upgrade risk indicator
  • Unused Code Share – Cleanup potential
  • Business Modifications – Should trend to zero

Apply Clean Core Using 2 Notions - Get Clean & Stay Clean :

Stay Clean

  • Avoid unnecessary extensions
  • Prefer Level A (BTP first)
  • Do not add technical debt

Get Clean

  • Identify unused code
  • Refactor Level D → C/B/A
  • Improve legacy step‑by‑step

Practical Improvement Examples

      

  • Replace User Exits (Level B) with released BAdIs (Level A)
  • Replace direct table access (e.g., MARA) with released APIs

👉Improves:

  • Upgrade stability
  • Cloud readiness
  • Technical depth score

Final Recommendations

  • Establish extensibility governance
  • Upskill developers (ABAP Cloud, BTP, SAP Build)
  • Define realistic, incremental goals
  • Prefer Side‑by‑Side on SAP BTP
  • Always aim for Level A first

Conclusion

Clean Core is not about removing customization, but about building extensions the right way. With the refined extensibility model, SAP provides clarity, flexibility, and measurable progress—enabling customers and partners to innovate safely while staying upgrade‑ready.

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Build Process Automation for Intelligent Invoice Extraction from Outlook
entry.content:
Imagine a world where your inbox doesn’t just store invoices, but actively processes them. No more manual downloads, no more mind-numbing data entry, and zero "human-error" bottlenecks.By integrating the "digital eyes" of SAP Document Information Extraction  with the orchestration power of SAP Build, organizations can now transform unstructured email attachments into clean, high-fidelity structured data in seconds. This blog explores the seamless end-to-end journey of an invoice—starting from the moment it hits an Outlook inbox, through its automated recognition and validation phase, to its final state of readiness for your core business systems.

Phase 1: Environment Readiness

The Local Bridge: The Desktop Agent 3.0 must be installed and registered to the tenant. This acts as the execution engine, enabling the cloud-based SAP Build environment to interact directly with the local Outlook application and file system.

-To set up the agent, follow this: Install and Set Up Desktop Agent 3 | SAP Tutorials

Phase 2: Agent Configuration: Creation, Assignment, and Project Binding

Once the agent is installed, it must be assigned to an environment within the SAP Build Lobby to authorize automation execution.

  • Create the Environment: Navigate to Control Tower > Environments and select Create. Provide a name, Identifier and click Create.

  • Register the Agent: Open the newly created environment and select Agent Management > Add Agent.

  • Define Agent Attributes: Navigate to Control Tower > Agent Attributes and select Create. Provide a name and choose the Type:

                        Free: For dynamic parameterization of values.

                        Predefined: For assigning specific, fixed values.

  • Assign Attribute to Agent: Under Control Tower > Agents, locate your agent and ensure it is connected. Select Manage Attributes, choose your newly created attribute, enter the corresponding value, and confirm.

  • Bind Attribute to Project: In your project’s Process Builder, navigate to Settings > Attributes. Add the same attribute and value, then save. This creates the final link, ensuring the project triggers specifically on your configured agent.

Phase 3: Building the Automation Logic

Now that the technical "handshake" is configured, we can begin building the automation. This phase focuses on creating a reliable bridge between your email inbox and your local storage.

Initializing the Automation

Start by creating a new automation in the Process Builder. This will be the engine that handles your file and email operations. Ensure you have the Outlook SDK and File System SDK added as dependencies in your project settings.

The following is the flow for extracting info from invoices, which includes opening Outlook, searching for specific emails, saving attachments, and processing the data:

Phase  4:Preparing the Workspace: Local and Outlook Setup

For the automation to successfully process documents, you must establish a specific "landing zone" for your files. This requires the following manual configurations:

1.Local File System: Manually create a folder on your computer at the path C:/temp/attachments. This folder acts as the dedicated directory where the bot will download and store email attachments before they are uploaded to the cloud or processed by the extraction logic.

2.Outlook Folder Configuration: Inside your Outlook application, you must create a new folder specifically named Invoices.

  • Setup for Testing: To test the flow, move an email containing an invoice attachment into this Invoices folder.
  • The Unread Status: Ensure the email is marked as Unread. The automation is designed to search for unread items to prevent processing the same invoice multiple times.

Phase 5: Configuring BTP Destinations

Destinations are secure connection points that allow your automation to communicate with external services. This is a two-step process: first creating them in the BTP Cockpit and then configuring them within SAP Build.

Creation in SAP BTP Cockpit

In your BTP Subaccount, navigate to Connectivity > Destinations and create the following:

1.Service & Extraction Destination: Create a destination to handle both internal routing for the Process Automation engine and the connection to the Document Information Extraction (DOX) service.

2.SAP Document Store Destination: Create a mandatory destination for  sap_process_automation_document_store. This allows the bot to move files from your local C:/temp/attachments folder into the secure SAP Document Management Service (DMS) for centralized cloud archiving.

Configuration under SAP Build

After creating them in BTP, you must explicitly link them to your project to establish the necessary communication channels:

Mapping: In your project settings, go to the Destinations tab. Map your BTP destinations to the corresponding project requirements. This step ensures that the automation knows exactly which BTP service to call for extraction and storage.

Phase 6: Deployment and Execution

With the logic built, folders prepared, and destinations mapped, the system is now a cohesive unit. The final step is to move the project from the design stage to active execution.

1.Releasing and Deploying

In the SAP Build Lobby, you must Release your project to create a version snapshot. Once released, navigate to the Deploy button. This sends the instructions to the cloud environment, where it waits for your local agent to "pick up" the task.

2.Monitoring

Once the automation completes, navigate to the Monitoring tab in SAP Build. Here, you can view the execution logs for your job.

grossAmount: The bot has correctly identified the total (e.g., $93.50).

filePath: The bot confirms where it stored the document locally (e.g., C:\temp\invoice-a (1).pdf).

recipient: It captures the specific email address the invoice was sent to.

3.The Final Flow in Action

The following is the automated flow created for extracting info from invoices:

Outlook Monitoring: The bot identifies an unread email in your manually created Invoices folder.

Local Storage: It downloads the attachment directly into C:/temp/attachments.

Data Extraction: Once the file is stored locally, the bot uses the extraction activity to communicate with the cloud. At this stage, we can successfully obtain the extracted info from the invoice.

Conclusion:

By linking Outlook to SAP Document Information Extraction through SAP Build, you transform the inbox from a storage bin into a high-speed data engine. This setup doesn't just save time—it eliminates manual errors and ensures your business systems receive accurate data instantly. It’s a simple yet powerful blueprint for turning administrative friction into a seamless, automated advantage.

Disclaimer:
All the views and opinions in this blog are my own and are made in my personal capacity. SAP shall not be responsible or liable for any of the contents published in this blog.
